risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All GP names were written on slips of paper and mixed in a container, and an administrative employee blinded to the aim of the trial drew names. The GP whose name was first drawn was assigned to the intervention group, the GP whose name was drawn second was assigned to the control group, and so on until all names were drawn." Comment: This describes a clearly random allocation procedure (drawing names from mixed slips of paper), which is an explicit random method and supports a truly random sequence.

Quote: "All GP names were written on slips of paper and mixed in a container, and an administrative employee blinded to the aim of the trial drew names. The GP whose name was first drawn was assigned to the intervention group, the GP whose name was drawn second was assigned to the control group, and so on until all names were drawn." Comment: This describes an allocation process that prevented the recruiters/GPs from foreseeing the upcoming assignment; the draw was done by an administrative employee, with names mixed in a container, before the sequence of assignments was revealed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Another limitation was absence of a blinded control arm. The control GPs were aware that their colleagues at the same PHCC were providing the intervention" and "The GPs randomised to the group that provided the intervention received four hours of guidance about how to include the results of MADRS-S in the person-centred consultation." Comment: Participants and personnel were not blinded; the paper explicitly states there was no blinded control arm and that control GPs knew about the intervention. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence key outcomes in this trial, especially depression symptoms, medication use, health care use, and sick leave, so the dominant performance-bias risk is high.

Summary: Outcome assessment was mainly by patient self-report questionnaires (BDI-II, EQ-5D, GHQ-12) collected by a study nurse at baseline and follow-ups, while some outcomes were obtained from electronic patient records. The report does not state that outcome assessors were blinded, and the key subjective outcomes were self-assessed by participants, so if participant blinding is not described, detection bias cannot be ruled out. Comment: For the self-reported depression, quality of life, and well-being outcomes, the patient is effectively the assessor; for EPR-based outcomes (prescriptions, sick leave, health care use) measurement is more objective, but the paper does not clearly describe blinding of the person extracting/handling these data.

Quote: "There was no significant difference in participation rate in the patients in the intervention and control groups at the 3-, 6- or 12-months follow-ups." Comment: The paper indicates similar follow-up participation across groups and does not report a clear imbalance in missing outcome data. It also states "All analyses were conducted on an intention to treat basis," which supports appropriate handling of missingness. Although dropouts occurred, the reported attrition pattern does not suggest differential, outcome-related loss sufficient to bias the results.
